[St. Peter's Basilica](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Basilica "St. Peter's Basilica"), Vatican City

[James Francis Edward Stuart's signature](/wiki/File:Signature_of_James_Francis_Edward_Stuart.svg "James Francis Edward Stuart's signature")

(10 June 1688 – 1 January 1766), also known as the **Old Pretender**, was the senior [House of Stuart](/wiki/House_of_Stuart "House of Stuart") claimant to the thrones of [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") and [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") from 1701 until his death in 1766. The only son of [James II of England](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") and his second wife, [Mary of Modena](/wiki/Mary_of_Modena "Mary of Modena"), he was [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales "Prince of Wales") and heir-apparent until his [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") father was deposed and exiled in the [Glorious Revolution of 1688](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution_of_1688 "Glorious Revolution of 1688"). His [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") half-sister [Mary II](/wiki/Mary_II "Mary II") and her husband [William III and II](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") became co-monarchs. As a Roman Catholic, he was subsequently excluded from the succession by the [Bill of Rights 1689](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689").

**James Francis Edward Stuart** (10 June 1688 – 1 January 1766), also known as the **Old Pretender**, was the senior [House of Stuart](/wiki/House_of_Stuart "House of Stuart") claimant to the thrones of [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") and [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") from 1701 until his death in 1766. The only son of [James II of England](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") and his second wife, [Mary of Modena](/wiki/Mary_of_Modena "Mary of Modena"), he was [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales "Prince of Wales") and heir-apparent until his [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") father was deposed and exiled in the [Glorious Revolution of 1688](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution_of_1688 "Glorious Revolution of 1688"). His [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") half-sister [Mary II](/wiki/Mary_II "Mary II") and her husband [William III and II](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") became co-monarchs. As a Roman Catholic, he was subsequently excluded from the succession by the [Bill of Rights 1689](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689").

James claimed the thrones of England, Ireland and Scotland when his father died in September 1701. As part of the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession"), in 1708 [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") of France backed a [landing in Scotland](/wiki/Planned_French_invasion_of_Britain_(1708) "Planned French invasion of Britain (1708)") on his behalf. This failed, as did further attempts in [1715](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1715 "Jacobite rising of 1715") and [1719](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1719 "Jacobite rising of 1719"), after which James lived quietly in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"). Led by his elder son [Charles Edward Stuart](/wiki/Charles_Edward_Stuart "Charles Edward Stuart"), the [1745 Rising](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1745 "Jacobite rising of 1745") was the last serious effort to restore the Stuart line.

## Birth and childhood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Francis_Edward_Stuart&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Birth and childhood")]

James Francis Edward Stuart was born on 10 June 1688, at [St. James's Palace](/wiki/St._James%27s_Palace "St. James's Palace"), first and only son of [James II of England](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") and his second wife, [Mary of Modena](/wiki/Mary_of_Modena "Mary of Modena"), both [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Catholics "Catholics").[[1]](#cite_note-brmon-1) As the eldest surviving son of the reigning monarch he was automatically [Duke of Cornwall](/wiki/Duke_of_Cornwall "Duke of Cornwall") and [Duke of Rothesay](/wiki/Duke_of_Rothesay "Duke of Rothesay") at birth, and was created [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales "Prince of Wales") in July 1688.[[2]](#cite_note-pow-ps-2)

His birth was unexpected, as it came five years after his mother's tenth pregnancy; none of the infants had survived more than a few days.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The birth reignited controversies of religion, as the new son would be raised a Roman Catholic. Wild rumours spread among British Anglicans: that the child had died [stillborn](/wiki/Stillborn "Stillborn"), and that the baby feted as the new prince was an impostor smuggled into the royal birth chamber in a [warming pan](/wiki/Warming_pan "Warming pan").[[4]](#cite_note-4) Protestants found it suspicious that everyone attending the birth was supposedly Roman Catholic,[[5]](#cite_note-keates-5) although the Protestant [Lady Bellasyse](/wiki/Baron_Belasyse "Baron Belasyse") testified that she "saw the child taken out of the bed with the navel string hanging to its belly".[[6]](#cite_note-6) Another rumour was that James II had not been the father; he was said to be impotent after a bout with [venereal disease](/wiki/Venereal_disease "Venereal disease") years earlier. In an attempt to quash these rumours, James published the testimonies of over seventy witnesses to the birth.[[7]](#cite_note-ODNB-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Jacques_Fran%C3%A7ois_%C3%89douard_Stuart%2C_Prince_of_Wales.jpg/250px-Jacques_Fran%C3%A7ois_%C3%89douard_Stuart%2C_Prince_of_Wales.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacques_Fran%C3%A7ois_%C3%89douard_Stuart,_Prince_of_Wales.jpg)

James Francis Edward as [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales "Prince of Wales")

The line of succession to the throne was thrust into doubt. James II's eldest legitimate daughters, [Mary](/wiki/Mary_II "Mary II") and [Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain"), had been raised as Protestants.[[5]](#cite_note-keates-5) British Protestants had expected Mary, from his father's first marriage, to succeed their father.[[9]](#cite_note-Exeter-9) This possibility had kept Protestants reasonably content, with his reign a temporary inconvenience. Now that Mary or Anne's succession was in doubt with this new Roman Catholic son and heir, discontent grew, already stoked by James II's actions which had alienated Tory Anglicans who had previously been inclined to honour him as sovereign even if they differed in religion. This movement would become the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution"); Mary's husband [William of Orange](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") landed in England, backed by an army of English and Scottish exiles, as well as Dutch soldiers. Much of the English army promptly defected to William's cause, causing James II and his family to flee rather than stay and fight.[[5]](#cite_note-keates-5)

On 9 December, Mary of Modena disguised herself as a laundress and escaped with the infant James to France. Young James was brought up at the [Château de Saint-Germain-en-Laye](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Saint-Germain-en-Laye "Château de Saint-Germain-en-Laye"),[[1]](#cite_note-brmon-1) which Louis XIV had turned over to the exiled James II. Both the ex-king and his family were held in great consideration by the French king (who was his first cousin), and they were frequent visitors at Versailles where Louis XIV and his court treated them as ruling monarchs.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In June 1692 James's sister [Louisa Maria](/wiki/Louisa_Maria_Stuart "Louisa Maria Stuart") was born.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

He later received a military education overseen by [Richard Hamilton](/wiki/Richard_Hamilton_(officer) "Richard Hamilton (officer)") and [Dominic Sheldon](/wiki/Dominic_Sheldon "Dominic Sheldon"), two veterans of the [Irish Army](/wiki/Irish_Army_(Kingdom_of_Ireland) "Irish Army (Kingdom of Ireland)").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECorp2009278-12)

## Struggle for the throne

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Francis_Edward_Stuart&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Struggle for the throne")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/James_Francis_Edward_Stuart_c._1703_attributed_to_Alexis_Simon_Belle.jpg/250px-James_Francis_Edward_Stuart_c._1703_attributed_to_Alexis_Simon_Belle.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_Francis_Edward_Stuart_c._1703_attributed_to_Alexis_Simon_Belle.jpg)

James Francis Edward c. 1703, portrait in the [Royal Collection](/wiki/Royal_Collection "Royal Collection") attributed to [Alexis Simon Belle](/wiki/Alexis_Simon_Belle "Alexis Simon Belle")

On his father's death in 1701, James was proclaimed as rightful king by [Louis XIV of France](/wiki/Louis_XIV_of_France "Louis XIV of France"), despite having previously recognised the legitimacy of [William III and II](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") under the 1697 [Treaty of Ryswick](/wiki/Treaty_of_Ryswick "Treaty of Ryswick"). Spain, the [Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States"), and [Modena](/wiki/Duchy_of_Modena_and_Reggio "Duchy of Modena and Reggio") also recognised him as king of [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") and [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") and refused to recognise William III and II, [Mary II](/wiki/Mary_II "Mary II"), or [Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain") as legitimate sovereigns. As a result of his claiming his father's lost thrones, James was [attainted](/wiki/Attainder "Attainder") for treason in London on 2 March 1702, and his titles were forfeited under English law.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Early attempts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Francis_Edward_Stuart&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early attempts")]

Though delayed in France by an attack of [measles](/wiki/Measles "Measles"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] James [attempted invasion](/wiki/Planned_French_invasion_of_Britain_(1708) "Planned French invasion of Britain (1708)"), trying to land at the [Firth of Forth](/wiki/Firth_of_Forth "Firth of Forth") on 23 March 1708. The fleet of Admiral Sir [George Byng](/wiki/George_Byng,_1st_Viscount_Torrington "George Byng, 1st Viscount Torrington") intercepted the French ships, which, combined with bad weather,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] prevented a landing.[[14]](#cite_note-Nottingham-14)

James served for a time as a volunteer in the French army, as his father had done during the interregnum.[[15]](#cite_note-quincy197-15) Between August and September 1710, Queen Anne appointed a new [Tory](/wiki/Tories_(British_political_party) "Tories (British political party)") administration led by [Robert Harley](/wiki/Robert_Harley,_1st_Earl_of_Oxford "Robert Harley, 1st Earl of Oxford"), who entered into a secret correspondence with [de Torcy](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Colbert,_marquis_de_Torcy "Jean-Baptiste Colbert, marquis de Torcy"), the [French Minister of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_and_European_Affairs_(France) "Ministry of Foreign and European Affairs (France)"), in which he claimed to desire James's accession to the throne should James convert to Protestantism.[[7]](#cite_note-ODNB-7) A year later, however, the British government pushed for James's expulsion from France as a precondition for a peace treaty with France. In accordance with the [Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht "Treaty of Utrecht") (1713), Harley and [Lord Bolingbroke](/wiki/Henry_St_John,_1st_Viscount_Bolingbroke "Henry St John, 1st Viscount Bolingbroke"), the Secretary of State, colluded with the French in exiling James to the [Duchy of Lorraine](/wiki/Duchy_of_Lorraine "Duchy of Lorraine").[[7]](#cite_note-ODNB-7)

Queen Anne became severely ill at Christmas 1713 and seemed close to death. In January 1714, she recovered but clearly had little time to live.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Through de Torcy and his London agent, Abbé François Gaultier, Harley maintained the correspondence with James and Bolingbroke entered into a separate correspondence with him. They both stated to James that his conversion to Protestantism would facilitate his accession. However, James, a devout Roman Catholic, replied to Torcy: "I have chosen my own course, therefore it is for others to change their sentiments."[[7]](#cite_note-ODNB-7) In March came James's refusal to convert, following which Harley and Bolingbroke reached the opinion that James's accession was not feasible, though they maintained their correspondence with him.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As a result, in August 1714, James's second cousin,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] the [Elector of Hanover](/wiki/Elector_of_Hanover "Elector of Hanover"), [George Louis](/wiki/George_I_of_Great_Britain "George I of Great Britain"), a German-speaking Lutheran[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] who was the closest Protestant relative of the now-deceased Queen Anne, became king of the recently created [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") as George I.[[14]](#cite_note-Nottingham-14) James denounced him, noting "we have beheld a foreign family, aliens to our country, distant in blood, and strangers even to our language, ascend the throne".[[17]](#cite_note-17)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Following George's coronation in October 1714, [major riots](/wiki/Coronation_riots "Coronation riots") broke out in provincial England.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### The Fifteen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Francis_Edward_Stuart&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The Fifteen")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/The_Old_Pretender_lands_in_Scotland%2C_1715.jpg/250px-The_Old_Pretender_lands_in_Scotland%2C_1715.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Old_Pretender_lands_in_Scotland,_1715.jpg)

James ("The Old Pretender") lands in Scotland after [Sheriffmuir](/wiki/Battle_of_Sheriffmuir "Battle of Sheriffmuir"). An 18th-century engraving.

In the following year, Jacobites started uprisings [in Scotland](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1715 "Jacobite rising of 1715") and [Cornwall](/wiki/Jacobite_uprising_in_Cornwall_of_1715 "Jacobite uprising in Cornwall of 1715") aimed at putting "James III and VIII" on the throne. On 22 December 1715, James reached Scotland after the Jacobite defeats at the [Battle of Sheriffmuir](/wiki/Battle_of_Sheriffmuir "Battle of Sheriffmuir") (13 November 1715) and [Battle of Preston (1715)](/wiki/Battle_of_Preston_(1715) "Battle of Preston (1715)").[[14]](#cite_note-Nottingham-14) He landed at [Peterhead](/wiki/Peterhead "Peterhead") and soon fell ill with fever, his illness made more severe by the icy Scottish winter.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In January 1716, he set up court at [Scone Palace](/wiki/Scone_Palace "Scone Palace"). Reputedly [Jane Stuart](/wiki/Jane_Stuart_(Quaker) "Jane Stuart (Quaker)"), a half-sister, came from [Wisbech](/wiki/Wisbech "Wisbech") in England to visit him.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Learning of the approach of government forces, he returned to France, sailing from [Montrose](/wiki/Montrose,_Angus "Montrose, Angus") on 5 February 1716. The abandonment of his rebel allies caused ill-feeling against him in Scotland;[[14]](#cite_note-Nottingham-14) nor was he welcomed on his return to France. His patron, [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV"), had died on 1 September 1715, and the French government found him a political embarrassment.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] When France, hitherto his main protector, [allied with Britain](/wiki/Anglo-French_Alliance_(1716%E2%80%931731) "Anglo-French Alliance (1716–1731)"), this effectively secured the [Hanoverian dynasty](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover")'s monarchy over the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Court-in-exile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Francis_Edward_Stuart&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Court-in-exile")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Jacobite_broadside_-_Coloured_portrait_of_Prince_James_as_young_man1.jpg/250px-Jacobite_broadside_-_Coloured_portrait_of_Prince_James_as_young_man1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacobite_broadside_-_Coloured_portrait_of_Prince_James_as_young_man1.jpg)

Coloured portrait of James as young man

After the unsuccessful invasion of 1715, James lived in [Papal territory](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States"), first at [Avignon](/wiki/Avignon "Avignon") (April 1716 – February 1717),[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBevan196792,_96-20) then at [Pesaro](/wiki/Pesaro "Pesaro") (1717)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBevan196799-21) and [Urbino](/wiki/Urbino "Urbino") (July 1717 – November 1718).[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBevan1967102,_110-22) Pope [Clement XI](/wiki/Clement_XI "Clement XI") offered James the [Palazzo Muti](/wiki/Palazzo_Muti "Palazzo Muti") or Palazzo del Re[[23]](#cite_note-23) in Rome as his residence, which he accepted. Pope [Innocent XIII](/wiki/Innocent_XIII "Innocent XIII"), like his predecessor, showed much support. Thanks to his friend Cardinal [Filippo Antonio Gualterio](/wiki/Filippo_Antonio_Gualterio_(cardinal) "Filippo Antonio Gualterio (cardinal)"), James was granted a life annuity of 12,000[[24]](#cite_note-24) Roman [scudi](/wiki/Roman_scudo "Roman scudo"). Such help enabled him to organise a Jacobite court at Rome, where, although he lived in splendour, he continued to suffer from fits of melancholy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Further efforts to restore the Stuarts to the British throne were planned. In 1719 a major expedition left Spain but was forced to turn back due to weather. A small landing took place in the Scottish Highlands, but the [Jacobite rising of 1719](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1719 "Jacobite rising of 1719") was defeated at the [Battle of Glen Shiel](/wiki/Battle_of_Glen_Shiel "Battle of Glen Shiel"). James had gone to Spain in the hope he could take part in the invasion, but following its abandonment was forced to return to Italy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A further attempt was planned in 1722,[[9]](#cite_note-Exeter-9) but following the exposure of the [Atterbury Plot](/wiki/Atterbury_Plot "Atterbury Plot") it came to nothing.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In exercise of his pretended position, James purported to create titles of nobility, referred to as [Jacobite peerages](/wiki/Jacobite_peerage "Jacobite peerage"), for his British supporters and members of his court, none of which have ever been recognised in Britain.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The court-in-exile became a popular stop for English travellers making a [Grand Tour](/wiki/Grand_Tour "Grand Tour"), regardless of political affiliation.[[25]](#cite_note-25) For many, it functioned as an unofficial consulate. Those in need of medical attention preferred being treated by one of their own countrymen. In 1735 court physicians tended to [Edmund Sheffield, 2nd Duke of Buckingham and Normanby](/wiki/Edmund_Sheffield,_2nd_Duke_of_Buckingham_and_Normanby "Edmund Sheffield, 2nd Duke of Buckingham and Normanby"), and thirty years later to [James Boswell](/wiki/James_Boswell "James Boswell").[[26]](#cite_note-Corp-26)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

James remained well-treated in Rome until his death. He was allowed to hold Protestant services at Court, and was given land where his Protestant adherents could receive a public burial.[[26]](#cite_note-Corp-26)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

### Marriage and progeny

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Francis_Edward_Stuart&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Marriage and progeny")]

[Louise Adélaïde d'Orléans](/wiki/Louise_Ad%C3%A9la%C3%AFde_d%27Orl%C3%A9ans "Louise Adélaïde d'Orléans") (*Mademoiselle d'Orléans*), daughter of [Philippe II, Duke of Orléans](/wiki/Philippe_II,_Duke_of_Orl%C3%A9ans "Philippe II, Duke of Orléans"), was at one time suggested as a wife for James, but nothing came of it.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In March 1717, while James was visiting [Modena](/wiki/Modena "Modena"), he became engaged to his cousin [Benedetta d'Este](/wiki/Benedetta_d%27Este "Benedetta d'Este"), but her father Duke [Rinaldo](/wiki/Rinaldo_d%27Este_(1655%E2%80%931737) "Rinaldo d'Este (1655–1737)") put an end to the engagement to preserve his relations with Hanover and Great Britain.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBevan196798–99,_103-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Maria_Klementyna_Sobieska_%281702-1735%29.jpg/250px-Maria_Klementyna_Sobieska_%281702-1735%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maria_Klementyna_Sobieska_(1702-1735).jpg)

James's wife, [Maria Clementina Sobieska](/wiki/Maria_Clementina_Sobieska "Maria Clementina Sobieska")

On 3 September 1719, James married [Maria Clementina Sobieska](/wiki/Maria_Clementina_Sobieska "Maria Clementina Sobieska") (1702–1735), granddaughter of King [John III Sobieski](/wiki/John_III_Sobieski "John III Sobieski") of Poland. The wedding was held in the chapel of the Episcopal Palace in [Montefiascone](/wiki/Montefiascone "Montefiascone"), near [Viterbo](/wiki/Viterbo "Viterbo"). By his wife he had two sons:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

1. [Charles Edward Stuart](/wiki/Charles_Edward_Stuart "Charles Edward Stuart") (31 December 1720 – 31 January 1788), nicknamed "Bonnie Prince Charlie"
2. [Henry Benedict Stuart](/wiki/Henry_Benedict_Stuart "Henry Benedict Stuart") (11 March 1725 – 13 July 1807), a [cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholic_Church) "Cardinal (Catholic Church)") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church")

### Bonnie Prince Charlie

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Francis_Edward_Stuart&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Bonnie Prince Charlie")]

Following James's failure, attention turned to his son Charles, "[the Young Pretender](/wiki/Charles_Edward_Stuart "Charles Edward Stuart")", who led the [major uprising](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1745 "Jacobite rising of 1745") of 1745. With the failure of this second rebellion, the Stuart hopes of regaining the British throne were effectively destroyed.[[28]](#cite_note-28) James and Charles later clashed repeatedly, and relations between them broke down completely when James played a role in the appointment of his son [Henry](/wiki/Henry_Benedict_Stuart "Henry Benedict Stuart") as a cardinal. Henry then took holy orders, infuriating Charles, who believed that such an obvious profession of their Catholicism damaged their chances of being restored.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Prince_James_Francis_Edward_Stuart_by_Anton_Raphael_Mengs.jpg/250px-Prince_James_Francis_Edward_Stuart_by_Anton_Raphael_Mengs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prince_James_Francis_Edward_Stuart_by_Anton_Raphael_Mengs.jpg)

Portrait of James from 1748

### Later years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Francis_Edward_Stuart&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Later years")]

After the 1745 rising, there were no other plots to restore the Stuart dynasty except for when, in 1759, the French government briefly considered a scheme to have James (then aged 70) crowned King of Ireland as part of [their plans to invade Britain](/wiki/Planned_French_invasion_of_Britain_(1759) "Planned French invasion of Britain (1759)"), but the offer was never formally made to James. Several separate plans also involved Charles being given control of a French-backed independent Ireland, though that too was aborted after Charles showed up at a meeting with the French to discuss the plan late, argumentative, and idealistic in expectations, so that the French dismissed the possibility of Jacobite assistance.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[31]](#cite_note-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Tomb_of_Stuart_in_the_Vatican.jpg/250px-Tomb_of_Stuart_in_the_Vatican.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tomb_of_Stuart_in_the_Vatican.jpg)

Tomb of James Francis Edward Stuart and his two sons in [St. Peter's Basilica](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Basilica "St. Peter's Basilica")

### Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Francis_Edward_Stuart&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Death")]

After a lingering illness, James died aged 77 on 1 January 1766, at his home, the Palazzo Muti in Rome,[[9]](#cite_note-Exeter-9)[[32]](#cite_note-libraryireland-32) and was buried in the crypt of [St. Peter's Basilica](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Basilica "St. Peter's Basilica") in present-day [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"). His grave is marked by the [Monument to the Royal Stuarts](/wiki/Monument_to_the_Royal_Stuarts "Monument to the Royal Stuarts"). His claimed reign had lasted for 64 years, 3 months and 16 days, longer than any British monarch until Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II")'s reign surpassed it on 23 May 2016.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## End of papal support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Francis_Edward_Stuart&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: End of papal support")]

Following James's death the pope refused to recognise the claim to the British and Irish thrones of his elder son Charles, which had severely exacerbated the hostility between England and the Catholic Church. Instead, from 14 January 1766, in stages over the following decade, Rome accepted the [Hanoverian dynasty](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover") as the legitimate rulers of Britain and Ireland; this was accompanied by a gradual relaxation and reform of the anti-Catholic ["penal laws"](/wiki/Penal_Laws_(Ireland) "Penal Laws (Ireland)") in Britain and Ireland.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Two months after James's death, on 14 March, the royal arms of England were removed from the doorway of the Palazzo Muti.[[32]](#cite_note-libraryireland-32) In 1792, the papacy specifically referred to [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") as the "King of Great Britain and Ireland", which elicited a protest from James's younger son [Henry](/wiki/Henry_Benedict_Stuart "Henry Benedict Stuart"), who was by then the Jacobite claimant.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Titles and honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Francis_Edward_Stuart&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Titles and honours")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Coat_of_Arms_of_the_Stuart_Princes_of_Wales_%281610-1688%29.svg/250px-Coat_of_Arms_of_the_Stuart_Princes_of_Wales_%281610-1688%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_Arms_of_the_Stuart_Princes_of_Wales_(1610-1688).svg)

Coat of arms of James Francis Edward Stuart as Prince of Wales

James was [Duke of Cornwall](/wiki/Duke_of_Cornwall "Duke of Cornwall") from birth.[[35]](#cite_note-35) He was created [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales "Prince of Wales") on 4 July 1688.[[36]](#cite_note-PoW_site-36) He lost his titles on his [attainder](/wiki/Attainder "Attainder") for treason in 1702.

### Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Francis_Edward_Stuart&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Honours")]

* *Jacobite*, **KG**: [Knight of the Garter](/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter "Order of the Garter"), *1692–1766*

### Arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Francis_Edward_Stuart&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Arms")]

As Prince of Wales, James bore a [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") consisting of those of the kingdom, differenced by a *label argent of three points*.[[37]](#cite_note-37)
